statement:
There is a table with $n$ rows and $18$ columns. Each of its cells contains a $0$ or a $1$. The table satisfies the following properties: 
ewline 1. Every two rows are different 
ewline 2. Each row contains exactly $6$ cells that contain $1$. 
ewline 3. For every three rows, there exists a column so that the intersection of the column with the three rows (the three cells) all contain $0$. 
ewline What is the greatest possible value of $n$?
Hint 1: $n=\binom{17}{6}$ is possible by fixing $0$ to a column
Hint 2: Prove that $n=\binom{17}{6}$ is the limit
Hint 3: Adding on the previous hint, consider all $\binom{18}{6}$ possible rows and figure out how to group them to form an upper bound

statement: Let $p$ be an odd prime and $a,b,c$ be integers so that the integers$$a^{2023}+b^{2023},\quad b^{2024}+c^{2024},\quad a^{2025}+c^{2025}$$are divisible by $p$. \newline Prove that $p$ divides each of $a,b,c$.
Hint 1: Treat this exactly lie a system of equations in $a, b, c$.
Hint 2: You can make your work simpler by assuming the statement isn't true and considering modular inverses.

statement: Find the smallest positive integer $m$ such that $5m$ is an exact $5^\text{th}$ power, $6m$ is an exact $6^\text{th}$ power, and $7m$ is an exact $7^\text{th}$ power.
Hint 1: look at the prime factorisation
Hint 2: to find the smallest such integers, remember that this means avoiding extra factors where we can

statement: Suppose that $f$ and $g$ are two functions defined on the set of positive integers and taking positive integer values. Suppose also that the equations $f(g(n)) = f(n) + 1$ and $g(f(n)) = g(n) + 1$ hold for all positive integers. Prove that $f(n) = g(n)$ for all positive integer $n.$
Hint 1: If you already had $f \equiv g$, what would stronger condition would follow?
Hint 2: The smallest elements of $f$ and $g$ are key, try to prove that they are the same.

statement:
For a positive integer \( n \geq 2 \), does there exist positive integer solutions to the following system of equations?

\[
\begin{cases} 
a^n - 2b^n = 1, \\
b^n - 2c^n = 1.
\end{cases}
\]
Hint 1: NT arguments don't really work here, try to find something simpler
Hint 2: $n$th powers are usually far from each other, how can you use that to get a contradiction?

statement:
Let $S$ be the set of positive real numbers. Find all functions $f\colon S^3 \to S$ such that, for all positive real numbers $x$, $y$, $z$ and $k$, the following three conditions are satisfied:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
\item $xf(x,y,z) = zf(z,y,x)$,
\item $f(x, ky, k^2z) = kf(x,y,z)$,
\item $f(1, k, k+1) = k+1$.
\end{enumerate}
Hint 1: get the value of $f(1,1,1)$, and from there and playing around try to guess the answer
Hint 2: try and make the 2nd condition "symmetrical" in terms of $k$, this will help prove the function can be shifted by multiplying
Hint 3: shifting will make the 3rd condition very strong, use this and quadratic formula to prove the answer

statement: Let $x_1$, $x_2$, $\ldots$, $x_{2025}$ be real numbers with $x_1^2+x_2^2+\cdots+x_{2025}^2=1$. Determine the maximum value of $$|x_1-x_2|+|x_2-x_3|+\cdots +|x_{2024}-x_{2025}|+|x_{2025}-x_1|$$
Hint 1: Try to prove the statement when you put an even number instead of 2025
Hint 2: The even case can be finished via AM-QM. Can you reduce the odd case to the even one?
Hint 3: What happens if $x_{r-1}\le x_r\le x_{r+1}$ for some $r$?
Solution: Take all indices $\mod n$\\ First some notation: Let $$s_k=\max \sum_{i=1}^k |x_i - x_{i+1}|$$ with $\sum_{i=1}^k x_i^2 =1$. Note that the given sum is cyclic. \\\\\emph{Key claim}. $s_{2n+1}=s_{2n}$. \\\\ First note that by choosing $x_{2n+1}=0$ and applying triangle inequality we get that $$s_{2n+1}$$$$\ge  \sum_{i=1}^{2n-1} |x_i - x_{i+1}|+ |x_{2n}|+|-x_1|\ge\sum_{i=1}^{2n-1} |x_i - x_{i+1}|+ |x_{2n}-x_1|=s_{2n}$$ Now note that for $s_{2n+1}$ there exist an index $r$ such that either $x_{r-1}\le x_r\le x_{r+1}$ or $x_{r-1}\ge x_r\ge x_{r+1}$ cuz else the sequence has to have even length. \\ WLOG choose $r=2n+1$ (due to cyclic). We get that $$s_{2n+1}=  \sum_{i=1}^{2n-1} |x_i - x_{i+1}| \pm (x_{2n}-x{2n+1}+x_{2n+1}-x_1)$$$$\le\sum_{i=1}^{2n-1} |x_i - x_{i+1}|+ |x_{2n}-x_1|=s_{2n}$$ So indeed $s_{2n+1}=s_{2n}$. \\\\ Now we simply calculate $s_{2n}$. I claim that this is $s_{2n}=2\sqrt{2n}$. \begin{align*} s_{2n}=\sum_{i=1}^{2n} |x_i - x_{i+1}| \overset{\text{\footnotesize AM-QM}}{\le} \sqrt{2n\sum_{i=1}^{2n} (x_i - x_{i+1})^2}\overset{!}{\le} 2\sqrt{2n\sum_{i=1}^{2n}x_i^2}\\\iff \sum_{i=1}^{2n}(x_i+x_{i+1})^2\ge 0 \end{align*} So we are done and $s_{2n+1}=2\sqrt{2n}$. Equality occurs at $$x_i=(-1)^i \frac{1}{\sqrt{2024}}, \quad 1\le i\le 2024$$ $$x_{2025}=0$$.

statement: Let $n$ be a fixed positive integer and consider an $n\times n$ grid of real numbers. Determine the greatest possible number of cells $c$ in the grid such that the entry in $c$ is both strictly greater than the average of $c$'s column and strictly less than the average of $c$'s row.
Hint 1: Think about the smallest element in every column and the greatest element in every row.
Hint 2: Colour the smallest element in every column red and the greatest element in every row blue. Can cells be red and blue at the same time? If so, are there any constraints?
Hint 3: Proof that we can't have two cells that are both red and blue.

statement: Determine all pairs of integers $(x, y)$ such that $2xy$ is a perfect square and $x^2 + y^2$ is a prime number.
Hint 1: Set $x^2+y^2=p$ and $2xy=k^2$ and add the equations
Hint 2: Use difference of squares
Hint 3: After using hint 2 you should get that $2x+2y=p+1$. Now use the fact that $p=x^2+y^2$.

statement: Let $n>1$ be a positive integer and $S$ the set of coprime positive integers to $n$, which are also smaller than $n$. Determine the average value of $S$.
Hint 1: Try a few small cases, what do you notice?
Hint 2: If $k$ is coprime to $n$ then so is $n-k$.

statement:
Prove that there exists a real number $\varepsilon>0$ such that there are infinitely many sequences of integers $0<a_1<a_2<\hdots<a_{2025}$ satisfying
\[\gcd(a_1^2+1, a_2^2+1,\hdots, a_{2025}^2+1) > a_{2025}^{1+\varepsilon}.\]
Hint 1: This problem seems to be asking us to construct infinitely many positive integers $N$ such that the $2025^{th}$ smallest integer $k$ satisfying $N | k^2 + 1$ is smaller than $N$ to the power of something less than 1. \newline But if we consider the prime factors of $N$ and use CRT, it is \textit{extremely} unpredictable. So how do we make the idea of ensuring $N | k^2 + 1$ more \textit{practical?}
Hint 2: Polynomials. We need to construct these numbers using polynomials and hope that $a_{2025}$ has an $O(x^i)$ trajectory and the gcd has an $O(x^j)$ trajectory where $i < j$.
Hint 3: Construct pairwise coprime nonlinear polynomials $P_1, P_2, \hdots P_k$ such that for any $i$, the product of $P_j$ for all $j = 1$ to $k$ \textit{except} $j = i$ is a polynomial with a degree-$0$ remainder modulo $P_i$. This will allow CRT to kill the problem.

statement: Let $ f: \bR \to \bZ_+$ be a function which satisfies \[ f\left(x + \dfrac{1}{f(y)}\right) = f\left(y + \dfrac{1}{f(x)}\right)\]for all $ x$, $ y\in\mathbb{R}$. Prove that there is a positive integer which is not a value of $ f$.
Hint 1: Prove that if $f(a)=f(b)$ then $f(a+r)=f(b+r)$ for any $r\in \bQ^+$.
Hint 2: Show that $f(x)=f(x+1)$ for all $x$.

statement: Let $f \colon \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be a bijective function. Does there always exist an infinite number of functions $g \colon \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ such that $f(g(x))=g(f(x))$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$?
Hint 1: Your solution will be mildly cursed, consider either zorn's lemma or something relating to the difference between countably infinite and uncountably infinite values.
Solution:
I will prove that in a bijection from $\RR \to \RR$, there are infinetly many 
    distinct cycles or infinite length paths of the form $f^n(m)$, note that the set 
    ${n, f(n), f(f(n)), \dots}$ is countably infinite and thus an infinite number of these sets 
    such that they are not subsets of each other must be needed to cover all the reals. Thus 
    we can define an infinite number of functions $g$ such that for one of the subsets ${n, f(n), \dots}$, $g(n)=f^{-1}(n)$ 
    and for all other values $g(n)=n$.

statement: Let $a$ and $b$ be positive integers such that $a! + b!$ divides $a!b!$. Prove that $3a \ge 2b + 2$.
Hint 1: If $a \leq b$, we can factor out $a!$ in both expressions.
Hint 2: We have $a!+b!=a!(1+b(b-1)\dots(a+1))$. What is $1+b(b-1)\dots(a+1)$ coprime to?

statement: Suppose there are three empty buckets and $n \ge 3$ marbles. Alice and Bob play a game. For the first turn, Alice distributes all the marbles into the buckets so that each bucket has at least one marble. Then Bob and Alice alternate turns, starting with Bob. On a turn, the current player may choose a bucket and take 1, 2, or 3 marbles from it. The player that takes the last marble wins. Find all $n$ such that Alice has a winning strategy, including what Alice's first move (distributing the marbles) should be for these $n$.
Hint 1: Prove a sequence $(a,b,c)$ with a property $x$ such that subtracting $1,2,3$ from it would not make it $x$ and removing $1,2,3$ from a sequence that does not have property $x$ makes it have property $x$
Hint 2: Prove that Bob wins when $(a,b,c)$ is congruent to $1,2,3 (mod 4)$ in some order in the first arrangement of marbles
Hint 3: Prove that Bob also wins when $(a,b,c)$ is congruent to $0,x,x (mod 4)$ in some order in the first arrangement of marbles

statement:
Let $f(x)$ be a polynomial with real coefficients of degree 2. Suppose that for some pairwise distinct real numbers $a,b,c$ we have:

\[f(a)=bc , f(b)=ac, f(c)=ab.\]Determine $f(a+b+c)$ in terms of $a,b,c$.
Hint 1: One way is to notice that we have three equations in terms of the three coefficients of the polynomial, so we can manually obtain the coefficients. For a better solution, though, turn to hint 2.
Hint 2: Consider $g(x) = xf(x)$. What can we say about this new polynomial?
Hint 3: $g(x) -abc$ has roots $a, b, c$.

Source: 1990 Baltic Way Mathematical Olympiad, P1 of 20
statement: Integers $1, 2, \ldots, n$ are written (in some order) on the circumference of a circle. What is the smallest possible sum of moduli of the differences of neighbouring numbers?
Hint 1: firstly, try a small value of $n$ (for example $n=7$), what is the best order you can get?
Hint 2: what about the configuration forces the total of the moduli of the differences to be a certain amount?
Hint 3: look at $1$ and $n$: what can we say about the differences between stuff ``between'' them on the circle?

statement: There is an alphabet with 2 letters and 100 words. It is known that if you write any finite combination of the 100 words in a row, it can be uniquely determined what words were written, and in what order. What is the minimal sum of the lengths of the words?
Hint 1: Define the "weight"of a word of length $l$ to be $1/2^l$.
Hint 2: The sum of weights is at most 1.
Hint 3: Only algebra can help you from here.

statement: Let $n$ be a positive integer. Ana and Banana play a game. Banana thinks of a function $f\colon\mathbb{Z}\to\mathbb{Z}$ and a prime number $p$. He tells Ana that $f$ is nonconstant, $p<100$, and $f(x+p)=f(x)$ for all integers $x$. Ana's goal is to determine the value of $p$. She writes down $n$ integers $x_1,\dots,x_n$. After seeing this list, Banana writes down $f(x_1),\dots,f(x_n)$ in order. Ana wins if she can determine the value of $p$ from this information. Find the smallest value of $n$ for which Ana has a winning strategy.
Hint 1: Prove that the set of numbers Ana chose cannot miss a residue modulo $p$ for two different primes smaller than $100$.
Hint 2: Take two primes $p,q$ for which the set is surjective modulo both $p$ and $q$. When can you build two functions $f_{1},f_{2}$ which coincide on the set and satisfy $f_{1}(x+p)=f_{1}(x),f_{2}(x+q)=f_{2}(x)$?

statement: Let $k$ be the circumcircle of $\triangle ABC$ and let $k_a$ be A-excircle .Let the two common tangents of $k,k_a$ cut $BC$ in $P,Q$.Prove that $\measuredangle PAB=\measuredangle CAQ$.
Hint 1: prove that $AI$ is an angle bisector of $\angle PAQ$, there are hidden cyclic quads
Solution: Let $ M $ be the midpoint of arc $ BC $ in $ k $ and let $ I_a $ be the A-excenter of $ \triangle ABC. $ Let $ \widetilde{P}, $ $ \widetilde{Q} $ be the intersection of $ k $ with the common tangent of $ k, $ $ k_a $ passing through $ P, $ $ Q, $ respectively. Since the tangent of $ k $ at $ M $ is parallel to $ BC, $ so $ I_aP $ $ \parallel $ $ M\widetilde{P} $ $ \stackrel{\text{Reim's thm}}{\Longrightarrow} $ $ A, $ $ I_a, $ $ P, $ $ \widetilde{P} $ are concyclic. Similarly, $ A, $ $ I_a, $ $ Q, $ $ \widetilde{Q} $ are concyclic, so $ \measuredangle PAI_a $ $ = $ $ \measuredangle P\widetilde{P}I_a $ $ = $ $ \measuredangle I_a\widetilde{Q}Q $ $ = $ $ \measuredangle I_aAQ . $

statement: Let $n$ be an integer with $n \geq 2$. Over all real polynomials $p(x)$ of degree $n$, what is the largest possible number of negative coefficients of $p(x)^2$?
Hint 1: try considering extreme possibilities, and play around with some polynomials
Hint 2: clearly the constant term and leading coefficient are nonnegative, try proving by contradiction that the other $2n-1$ coefficients cannot all be nonnegative

statement: In terms of the real number \(a\), determine all real numbers \(x\) that satisfy \[\sqrt{a^2 - x^2} + \sqrt{2ax - x^2} > a.\]

Let $ABCD$ be a parallelogram with $|AD|=|BD|$. $P$ and $Q$ are points such that $\triangle APD$ and $\triangle CDQ$ are equilateral and completely outside the parallelogram. Prove that $\angle PQD=30^\circ$.
Hint 1: There are a lot of equal lengths, try to find congruent triangles.
Hint 2: Prove that $\triangle BPQ$ is equilateral.
Solution: We have $\angle BAP=60^\circ+\angle BAD=60^\circ+\angle ABD=60^\circ+\angle BDC=\angle BDQ$. Also we know $|BA|=|CD|=|DQ|$ and $|AP|=|AD|=|BD|$. Combining this gives $\triangle BAP\cong \triangle QDB$. So in particular $|BQ|=|BP|$. Let $\angle CDB=\angle ABD=\angle BAD=\alpha$. Then we know $\angle QDB=60^\circ+\alpha$. Also $\angle ADB=180^\circ-2\alpha$. So $\angle PDQ=360^\circ-\angle QDB-\angle ADB-\angle PDA= 360^\circ-60^\circ-\alpha-180^\circ+2\alpha-60^\circ=60^\circ+\alpha=\angle QDB$.  Together with $|QD|=|QD|$ and $|BD|=|PD|$ this implies $\triangle BQD\cong \triangle PQD$ and therefore $|BQ|=|PQ|$. So we have found $|BQ|=|BP|=|PQ|$ which means $\triangle BPQ$ is equilateral. $QD$ is a symmetry axis in this triangle, so $\angle BQD=\frac{\angle BQP}{2}=30^\circ$ and that is what we wanted to prove.

statement: A semicircle is erected over the segment $AB$ with center $M$. Let $P$ be one point different from $A$ and $B$ on the semicircle and $Q$ the midpoint of the arc of the circle $AP$. The point of intersection of the straight line $BP$ with the parallel to $P Q$ through $M$ is $S$. Prove that $PM = PS$ holds.
Hint 1: Note that $PM$ is a radius, so you want to prove that $PS$ has the length of the radius too
Hint 2: Prove that $PSMQ$ is a parallelogram. Why does this finish?

statement: Let $n$ be a natural number. A sequence is $k-$complete if it contains all residues modulo $n^k$. Let $Q(x)$ be a polynomial with integer coefficients. For $k\ge 2$, define $Q^k(x)=Q(Q^{k-1}(x))$, where $Q^1(x)=Q(x)$. Show that if $$0,Q(0),Q^2(0),Q^3(0),\ldots $$is $2018-$complete, then it is $k-$complete for all positive integers $k$.
Hint 1: Analyse the behaviour of Q using arrows
Hint 2: Hensel's Lemma may be helpful for dealing mod prime powers

statement: A point $D$ is chosen on the side $AC$ of a triangle $ABC$ with $\angle C < \angle A < 90^\circ$ in such a way that $BD=BA$. The incircle of $ABC$ is tangent to $AB$ and $AC$ at points $K$ and $L$, respectively. Let $J$ be the incenter of triangle $BCD$. Prove that the line $KL$ intersects the line segment $AJ$ at its midpoint.
Hint 1: Construct the reflection of $A$ in $L$.

statement: Find all functions $f \colon \mathbb{R}_{> 0} \to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ satisfying \[ \big(f(x)+f(y)\big)^3=xf(x)+yf(y)+3f(xy)\big(f(x)+f(y)\big) \] for all positive real numbers $x$ and $y$.
Hint 1: The solution space is very odd, don't  try to find it before solving the problem.
Hint 2: The initial ideas are all to just spam quadratics.
Solution:
Let $P(x, y)$ denote the assertion.
  \newline $P(1, 1)$
  \[f(1)(4f(1)^2-3f(1)-1)\]
  Clearly we get $f(1)=0$ or $f(1)=1$. 
  If $f(1)=0$, we get all values of $f(x)$ have the property $f(x)\in \{0, \frac{3=\sqrt{9+4x}}{2}\}$, clearly we get 
  that if $f(x)\neq 0$ then $f(x^2)\neq 0$. So there must be infinetly many values which are not zero. However $P(x, x)$ where $f(x)\neq 0$ clearly has only 
  finetly many solutions. Thus if $f(1)=0$, we get $f(x)=0$ for all $x$. Now suppose $f(1)=1$.
  \newline $P(x, 1)$
  \[f(x)^3-xf(x)=0\]
  Thus we get $f(x)\in \{0, \sqrt{x}\}$, Let $a, c$ be values such that $f(a)=\sqrt{a}$ and $b$ be a value such that $f(b)=0$.
  \newline $P(a, c)$
  \[3a\sqrt{c}+3c\sqrt{a}=f(ac)(\sqrt{a}+\sqrt{c})\]
  Let $S$ be the set of values such that for all $i\in S$, $f(i)=\sqrt{i}$, the above clearly gives that if $a, c\in S$, $ac\in S$.
  \newline $P(a, b)$
  \[x^{\frac{3}{2}}=x^{\frac{3}{2}}+f(ab)(\sqrt{a})\]
  Clearly this gives us that if $a\in S$ and $b\notin S$ that $ab\notin S$. Thus we get that if $x\in S$ that $\frac{1}{x} \in S$, so $S$ is a group closed under 
  multiplication.

statement: Let $n > 1$ be an integer. The numbers $1, 2, \ldots, n$ are written on a board. Aliceurill and Bobasaur take turns circling an uncircled number on the board, with Aliceurill going first. When the product of the circled numbers becomes a multiple of $n$, the game ends and the last player to have circled a number loses. For which values of $n$ can Bobasaur guarantee victory?
Hint 1: Rather than being greedy (choosing numbers coprime to $n$), Aliceurill should try to put Bobasaur in a tough spot.
Hint 2: By circling a number which is close to being a multiple of n, Aliceurill simplifies the game to some numbers being instant losses. How many of these are there?
Hint 3: For $n$ squarefree odd, Bobasaur has a simple strategy.

statement: Bugs Bunny plays a game in the Euclidean plane. At the $n$-th minute $(n \geq 1)$, Bugs Bunny hops a distance of $F_n$ in one of the $4$ cardinal directions, where $F_n$ is the $n$-th Fibonacci number (defined by $F_1 = F_2 =1$ and $F_n = F_{n-1} + F_{n-2}$ for $n \geq 3$). If the first two hops were perpendicular, prove that Bugs Bunny can never return to where he started.

A rectangular sheet of paper is folded so that one corner lies on top of the corner diagonally opposite. The resulting shape is a pentagon whose area is 20\% one-sheet-thick, and 80\% two-sheets-thick. Determine the ratio of the two sides of the original sheet of paper.
Hint 1: Prove that the two-sheets-thick portion of the pentagon unfolds into a rhombus whose side length is eight ninths of the longer side of the original sheet of paper.

Source: July 2010 Sharp Calculator Competition, P3 of 3
statement: The squares of a rectangular grid are coloured alternatively black and white in a chess board fashion. Each square contains an integer. The sum of the numbers in each row and the sum of the numbers in each column is even. Prove that the sum of all the numbers in the black squares is even.
Hint 1: you can add together the numbers in a row and the numbers in a column, but remember to count the square that is in both of them twice
Hint 2: cleverly select some rows and columns in a way that, when you take this sum, the parity is only affected by black squares
Hint 3: make black squares count an odd number of times and make white squares count an even number of times

statement:
Let $a, b, c$ be positive integers such that $b>a>1$ and $\gcd(c, ab)=1$. Prove that there exists a positive integer $n$, such that
\[ c \mid \binom{b^n} {a^n}. \]
Hint 1: Try and build up an $n$ by first splitting $c$ into prime powers.
Hint 2: First find an $n$ where the expression is divisible by $p$, then build up to $p^2,p^3$ etc. The idea is somewhat similar to Hensel's Lemma

statement:
Define a ring in the plane to be the set of points at a distance of at least $r$ and at most $R$ from a specific point $O$, where $r<R$ are positive real numbers. Rings are determined by the three parameters $(O, R, r)$. The area of a ring is labeled $S$. A point in the plane for which both its coordinates are integers is called an integer point.

a) For each positive integer $n$, show that there exists a ring not containing any integer point, for which $S>3n$ and $R<2^{2^n}$.

b) Show that each ring satisfying $100\cdot R<S^2$ contains an integer point.
Hint 1: For part a, use the characterization of integers which can be represented as a sum of two dquares

statement:
Circles $W_1,W_2$ intersect at $P,K$. $XY$ is common tangent of two circles which is nearer to $P$ and $X$ is on $W_1$ and $Y$ is on $W_2$. $XP$ intersects $W_2$ for the second time in $C$ and $YP$ intersects $W_1$ in $B$. Let $A$ be intersection point of $BX$ and $CY$. Prove that if $Q$ is the second intersection point of circumcircles of $ABC$ and $AXY$
\[\angle QXA=\angle QKP\]
Hint 1: there are many different inversion solutions, but the problem is screaming spiral similarity and miquel stuff, since both $Q$ and $K$ are centers of spiral similarities so we will focus on this approach. consider how to exploit the spiral at $Q$ or what happens if u consider where other points go.
Hint 2: one way to continue is to construct midpoints of $BX$ and $CY$. they do well as part of the spiral similarity
Hint 3: alternatively another way to exploit the spiral similarity is to consider where $K$ goes under the spiral at $Q$
Solution:
Let $K$ go to $L$ under the spiral at $Q$\\
Claim: $BPCL$ is cyclic\\
Proof\\
$\measuredangle BLC = \measuredangle XKY = \measuredangle BPC$ where the last follows by the fact that $P$ is the dumpty point in $XKY$\\

Claim 2: $P-K-L$\\
Proof\\
$\measuredangle BPL = \measuredangle BCL = \measuredangle XYK = \measuredangle BPK$ where the last line again follows from the fact that $P$ is the dumpty point.\\

Now $\measuredangle QKP = \measuredangle QKL = \measuredangle QXA$. \\

statement: Does there exist a function $f\colon \mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ such that for any $x>y,$ it satisfies $f(x)-f(y)\ge \sqrt{x-y}.$
Hint 1: Try to telescope the inequality

statement:
Show that the following equation is true for all positive integers \(a\) and \(b\):
\[\sum^{b-1}_{k=0}{\left\lfloor \frac{ka}{b} \right\rfloor} = \sum^{a-1}_{k=0}{\left\lfloor \frac{kb}{a} \right\rfloor}\]
Hint 1: You can assume \(a\) and \(b\) to be coprime. (Why?)
Hint 2: Notice how the residues of \(ka\) modulo \(b\) are different for all \(k\) from 0 to \(b-1\) (Why?). How can we directly calculate the left expression?
Hint 3: Let \(\lfloor x \rfloor = x - \{x\}\). Using Hint 2, how can we calculate \(\{x\}\) on the left side?

statement: Starting from the number 1 we write down a sequence of numbers where the next number in the sequence is obtained from the previous one either by doubling it, or by rearranging its digits (not allowing the first digit of the rearranged number to be 0). For instance we might begin: \[1, 2, 4, 8, 16, 61, 122, 212, 424, \ldots\] Is it possible to construct one that ends with the number 9,876,543,210? Is it possible to construct such a sequence that ends with the number 1,000,000,000?
Hint 1: For the first question, note that the sum of digits remains constant when rearranging a number's digits.  For the second question, note that 512 can be rearranged into 125.
Hint 2: Exploit the rule for divisibility by 9.

statement: Find all positive integers $n$ such that the number \[A=\sqrt{\frac{9n-1}{n+7}}\] is rational.
Hint 1: first let the expression equal $\frac ab$ and assume $a$ and $b$ are coprime
Hint 2: how can we get a finite list of possibilities for $a$ and $b$?
Hint 3: you should have the equation $n=\frac{7a^2+b^2}{9b^2-a^2}$, try to simplify the fraction, and then use the fact that $a$ and $b$ are coprime

statement:
Prove that among any $9$ distinct real numbers, there exist $4$ distinct numbers $a,b,c,d$ such that
\[(ac+bd)^2\ge\frac{9}{10}(a^2+b^2)(c^2+d^2)\]
Hint 1: Looking at the expressions $ac+bd$ as well as $a^2+b^2$ and $c^2+d^2$, what does it remind you of? We need something close to the equality case of Cauchy Schwarz.
Hint 2: Consider vectors $(a,b)$ and $(c,d)$. What does the condition say about these two vectors? More specifically, what does it say about the angle between them?
Hint 3: Try to use a pigeonhole argument. Note that we can take a vector and either swap its coordinates, or take the negative of both coordinates. The argument is slightly nontrivial, you will need to construct disjoint vectors which all have a certain property

statement: Let $k$ be a positive integer. Find all collection of integers $(a_1, a_2,\cdots, a_k)$ such that there exist a non-linear polynomial $P$ with integer coefficients, so that for all positive integers $n$ there exist a positive integer $m$ satisfying: $$P(n+a_1)+P(n+a_2)+...+P(n+a_k)=P(m)$$
Hint 1: Try to approximate $m$.
Hint 2: Show that $k$ must be a perfect power. This can be done in many ways through this approximation.

statement: Let $P(x)$ be a polynomial of degree $n > 1$ with integer coefficients and let $k$ be a positive integer. Consider the polynomial\[ Q(x) = P(P(\dots P(P(x)) \dots )) \] where $P$ occurs $k$ times. Prove that there are at most $n$ integers $t$ such that $Q(t) = t$.
Hint 1: Does this result hold for real polynomials?
Hint 2: Spam $a-b\mid P(a)-P(b)$
Solution:
Let $Q(x) = P^k(x)$. Suppose $Q$ has $n+1$ fixed points $a_1,\dots,a_n$, i.e. $Q(a_i)=a_i$ for $i\in \{1, 2, \dots, n+1\}$. We have that 
    $a_i-a_j\mid P(a_i)-P(a_j)\mid \dots \mid P^{k-1}(a_i)-P^{k-1}(a_j)\mid a_i-a_j$, thus $P(a_i)-P(a_j)=\pm(a_i-a_j)$ for all $i, j$. Now suppose there are indices $h,i,j$ with $h \ne j$ and $i \ne j$ such that one pair uses the $-$ and one uses the $+$; in other words suppose 
    that $P(a_h)-P(a_j)=a_j-a_h$ and $P(a_i)-P(a_j)=a_i-a_j$. We get $P(a_h)-P(a_i)=a_h+a_i-2a_j = \pm (a_h-a_i)$ which implies that $a_j=a_h$ or $a_j=a_i$ which 
    is a contradiction. Thus we have that $P(a_i)=a_i+c$ for a fixed $c$ or $P(a_i)=c-a_i$ for a fixed $c$; in both cases lagrange interpolation implies $P$ is linear which 
    is a contradiction.

Hint 1: Try replacing 2021 with smaller odd numbers to guess the bound and construction.
Hint 2: For the bound, consider that the sideways displacement of the piece is limited. Starting at the leftmost column, try to get to the rightmost and second rightmost columns.
Hint 3: For the construction, how can we control the movement of the piece better? Try painting the cells such that all initial conditions can eventually converge onto the same cell.

statement: Let $x,m,a,s$ be positive real numbers such that $x,m,a,s<1$. Prove that $$\frac{x^4}{m^3+a^2+s}+\frac{m^4}{a^3+s^2+x}+\frac{a^4}{s^3+x^2+m}+\frac{s^4}{x^3+m^2+a}$$ $$> \frac{x^3+m^3+a^3+s^3}{3}$$

Alice and the Mad Hatter are playing a game.  At the start of the game, three \(2024\)'s are written on the blackboard.  Then, Alice and the Mad Hatter alternate turns, with the Mad Hatter starting.  On the Mad Hatter's turn, he must pick one of the numbers on the blackboard and increase it by \(1\).  On Alice's turn, she must:
\begin{itemize}
    \item pick one of the numbers on the blackboard and decrease it by \(1\), and then
    \item replace the two numbers \(a\) and \(b\) on the blackboard which were not chosen by the Mad Hatter on the previous turn with \(\sqrt{ab}\).
\end{itemize}
Alice wins if, on the start of her turn, any of the three numbers are less than \(1\).

Can the Mad Hatter prevent Alice from winning?
Hint 1: Consider the sum of the three numbers.
Hint 2: Show that the sum decreases by at least \(\sqrt{x+1} - \sqrt{x}\) each turn for some \(x\), and then try to bound \(x\).

statement: Find all real numbers $a,b$ for which there exists a function $f \colon \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ such that for any real numbers $x,y$, $f(x+y)=ax+by$
Hint 1: Try plugging in values and testing things out. What happens when you do $y = 0$?
Hint 2: How can you apply the result from $y = 0$ to the original equation?
Solution:
the answer is all reals $a=b$. \\
firstly, by setting $f(x) = ax$ this is clearly true. \\
\\
let $y = -x$. \\
so we have $$f(x - x) = ax - bx \iff f(0) = x(a-b).$$
so $x(a-b)$ is constant for all $x\in\mathbb{R}$. \\
therefore $a-b$ = 0, so $a=b$. \\

Hint 1: When it comes to isogonal conjugates, pedal circles are very helpful
Hint 2: Note that the circumcenter condition, after homothety, is kind of an "anti-pedal" polygon. Chase how the angles move around to show $P$ and $Q$ are similar
Hint 3: To prove the second part, we want to show the ratios of similarity are the same, which we can prove by chasing lengths

statement: In a given community of people, each person has at least two friends within the community. Whenever some people from this community sit on a round table such that each adjacent pair of people are friends, it happens that no non-adjacent pair of people are friends. Prove that there exist two people in this community such that each has exactly two friends and they have at least one common friend.
Hint 1: Interpet it as a graph and look at a maximal path
Hint 2: What can you say about the edges of the path? Can you find a different maximal path and apply the same thing to it?

statement: Let $\mathbb{N}$ denote the set of positive integers. Find all functions $f \colon \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Z}$ such that\[\left\lfloor \frac{f(mn)}{n} \right\rfloor=f(m)\]for all positive integers $m,n$.
Hint 1: The floor is just an inequality, so write everything down in terms of it.
Hint 2: Use symmetry, what do you gain from it?
Hint 3: Try to calculyte the "slope", so $g(n)=\frac{f(n)}{n}$ and find its limit as $n\to \infty$

statement:
Integers $n$ and $k$ are given, with $n\ge k\ge2$. You play the following game against an evil wizard.

The wizard has $2n$ cards; for each $i=1,\ldots,n$, there are two cards labeled $i$. Initially, the wizard places all cards face down in a row, in unknown order.

You may repeatedly make moves of the following form: you point to any $k$ of the cards. The wizard then turns those cards face up. If any two of the cards match, the game is over and you win. Otherwise, you must look away, while the wizard arbitrarily permutes the $k$ chosen cards and then turns them back face-down. Then, it is your turn again.

We say this game is winnable if there exist some positive integer $m$ and some strategy that is guaranteed to win in at most $m$ moves, no matter how the wizard responds.

For which values of $n$ and $k$ is the game winnable?
Hint 1: think about a way to find the exact value of a card for more than one turn, and how can we repeatedly do this? Also don't let the $n=2$ case be misleading.
Hint 2: notice if we know the number on more than $n$ cards we are done by pigeonhole. so the answer is only when $n=k$.
Hint 3: in order to win, we need to know the value of $2$ cards that have the same number. however, is $n$ are shuffled each turn, what do we know after each turn?
Solution:
The game is winnable if and only if $n \neq k$.

First suppose $2 \le k < n$. Query the cards in positions $\left\{ 1, \dots, k \right\}$, then $\left\{ 2, \dots, k+1 \right\}$, and so on, up to $\left\{ 2n-k+1, 2n \right\}$. By taking the diff of any two adjacent queries, we can deduce for certain the values on cards $1, 2, \dots, 2n-k$. If $k \le n$, this is more than $n$ cards, so we can find a matching pair.

For $k = n$ we remark the following: at each turn after the first, assuming one has not won, there are $n$ cards representing each of the $n$ values exactly once, such that the player has no information about the order of those $n$ cards. We claim that consequently the player cannot guarantee victory. Indeed, let $S$ denote this set of $n$ cards, and $\overline S$ the other $n$ cards. The player will never win by picking only cards in $S$ or $\overline S$. Also, if the player selects some cards in $S$ and some cards in $\overline S$, then it is possible that the choice of cards in $S$ is exactly the complement of those selected from $\overline S$; the strategy cannot prevent this since the player has no information on $S$. This implies the result.

Source: The 5th International Olympiad of Metropolises, P2 of 6
statement: Does there exist an integer $n$ such that all its digits (in the decimal system) are greater than 5, while all the digits of $n^2$ are less than 5?
Hint 1: What's the smallest possible value of $n$ and the largest possible value of $n^2$?

Source: 2024 South African TSTST test 1, P2 of 5
statement: Let $ABCD$ be a convex quadrilateral with a right angle at the vertex $C$. Let $P$ be on segment $CD$, such that $\angle APD = \angle BPC$ and $\angle BAP = \angle ABC$. Prove that $2BC =AP + BP$
Hint 1: what does the 2nd given angle condition motivate?
Hint 2: this angle condition gives that if we extend $AP$ and $BC$ we get an isosceles triangle
Hint 3: find another isosceles triangle

statement:
Determine all positive integers $n$ for which there exist positive integers $a$, $b$, and $c$ satisfying
\[
2a^n + 3b^n = 4c^n.
\]
Hint 1: Clearly $n=1$ works. Assume $n>1$, and try taking modulo
Hint 2: If you work modulo 2 and 4, you can see you have to consider $n=2$ separately. Now suppose $n>2$
Hint 3: By Fermat's infinite descent principle, you can see that there will be no solutions for $n>2$. Make a similar argument for $n=2$.

statement: Suppose there are 18 lighthouses on the Persian Gulf. Each of the lighthouses lightens an angle with size 20 degrees. Prove that we can choose the directions of the lighthouses such that whole of the blue Persian (always Persian) Gulf is lightened.
Hint 1: Notice that you can move the light houses to any point in the region, which they are lighting. You want to put the different light sources together to one again.
Hint 2: Reverse the process. Note that you dont need to put together all at once but can make stops on the way.
Hint 3: To prove that this always works, apply discrete intermediate value theorem, so that in the reverse process you can split up your arc again into two arcs both containing the same number of lighthouses as sectors.

statement:
$n > 1$ stones were placed in different cells of an infinite square grid. Each day, all stones are removed, and new stones are placed in the cells $c$ satisfying the following condition: \\
\\ ``The number of stones present in either $c$, the cell below $c$, or the cell to the left of $c$ on the previous day is at least 2."\\
\\
Prove that after $n$ days no stones will be left in the grid.
Hint 1: Try small cases.
Hint 2: Consider "triangles": the shape formed by the cells $(x,y)$ satisfying $x\leq A$, $y\leq B$, $x+y\geq C$ for some $A,B,C$. What happens if the initial configuration of stones is contained in such a triangle?
Hint 3: Prove that it's possible to cover the initial configuration with non-touching triangles, whose side lengths sum up to $\leq n$.

statement:
Suppose that $C$ is a positive integer and $a_1,a_2,a_3,\hdots$ is an infinite sequence of positive integers satisfying
$$a_{n+1}=\sqrt{a_n^3-Ca_n}$$for all positive integers $n$. Prove that this sequence must be eventually constant, i.e. there exists a positive integer $N$ such that $a_N = a_{N+1} = a_{N+2} = \hdots$.
Hint 1: Consider $\nu_p$.

statement: Let $A$ be a $101$-element subset of the set $S=\{1,2,\ldots,1000000\}$. Prove that there exist numbers $t_1$, $t_2, \ldots, t_{100}$ in $S$ such that the sets \[ A_j=\{x+t_j\mid x\in A\},\qquad j=1,2,\ldots,100 \] are pairwise disjoint.
Hint 1: If you arbitrarily pick some element, what elements can you no longer pick?
Hint 2: Count the elements of $S$ you can no longer select by looking at the differences $a_i-a_j$ and disallowing elements of $S$ that have the same difference with an element $t_k$ that has already been selected.
Hint 3: You should get that 10100 are disallowed at each step, and 1 is selected, so there are 10101 less remaining elements to choose from.

statement: Let $AB$ be a diameter of the circle $k$. Let $t$ be the tangent to $k$ at point $B$ and $C, D$ be two points on $t$, so that $B$ lies between $C$ and $D$. The lines $AC$ and $AD$ cut $k$ again at points $E$ and $F$ respectively. The lines $DE$ and $CF$ cut again $k$ at points $G$ and $H$ respectively . Prove that the segments $AG$ and $AH$ have the same length.
Hint 1: Power of a Point will be extremely useful here
Hint 2: Prove $CEFD$ is cyclic
Solution: Claim 1: $CEFD$ cyclic.  Proof: Observe that from Power of a Point and the Pythagorean Theorem, $$CE \cdot CA = CB^2 = CA^2 - AB^2$$ $$ \implies CA(CA-CE) = AB^2$$ $$ \implies CA \cdot EA = AB^2.$$Similarly,$$AF \cdot AD = AB^2.$$Therefore,$$AE \cdot AC = AF \cdot AD$$so $CEFD$ is cyclic. $\square$  Now observe that$$\angle AGH$$ $$ = \angle AFH $$ $$= \angle AFC $$ $$= \pi - \angle CFD$$ $$ = \pi - \angle CED \text{ (This is where we use Claim 1) }$$ $$= \angle AED $$ $$= \angle AEG$$ $$ = \angle AHG,$$so $AG = AH$. $\square$

statement: Joe the Jaguar is on an infinite grid of unit squares, starting at the centre of one of them. At the $k$-th minute, Joe must jump a distance of $k$ units in any direction. For which $n$ is it possible for Joe to be inside or on the edge of the starting square after $n$ minutes?
Hint 1: To figure out what the answer should be, you can just imagine different possible paths for different values of $n$. There are several ways to prove the existence of such a path for the values for which it is possible, some easier than others: try to think of a simple construction.
Hint 2: To prove existence of such a path for the values of $n$ for which it is possible, you could use induction.

statement: William was bored at the math lesson, so he drew a circle and $n\ge3$ empty cells around the circumference. In every cell he wrote a positive number. Later on he erased the numbers and in every cell wrote the geometric mean of the numbers previously written in the two neighboring cells. Show that there exists a cell whose number was not replaced by a larger number.
Hint 1: suppose every cell gets replaced by a larger number, what would happen?
Hint 2: look at the geometric mean of all the numbers

Source: 2013 Sharygin Geometry Olympiad, Grade 10, P4 of 8
statement: Given a square cardboard of area $\tfrac{1}{4}$, and a paper triangle of area $\tfrac{1}{2}$ such that the square of its sidelength is a positive integer. Prove that the triangle can be folded in some ways such that the square can be placed inside the folded figure so that both of its faces are completely covered with paper.
Hint 1: Prove that the triangle's vertices can be placed on integer coordinate points on the Cartesian plane. This can be either done via geometric methods, or using Heron's formula and characterising all positive integer solutions to the quadratic.
Hint 3:
Consider the half-lattice formed by the lines of the form $x=\tfrac a2$ or $y=\tfrac b2$ for integers $a,b$. Point 1 is proven via Pick's Theorem on this half-lattice.

For point 2, for two points $U$ and $V$ to be able to land on the same spot, either $U+V$ or $U-V$ must have integer coordinates.

For the $U+V$ case, consider their midpoint, and for the $U-V$ case, consider the translation of the entire triangle by vectors $\pm\overrightarrow{UV}$ and its intersection with the original triangle.

statement: A set $X$ of integers is called $\textbf{crazy}$ if for all positive integers $d$ there exists a polynomial $P \in \mathbb{Z}[x]$ such that $\deg(P) \geq d$ and for all $n \in X $ there is some integer $m$ which satisfies $P(m)=n$. Are there any infinite crazy sets?
Hint 1: The answer is yes. You need to find a sequence of polynomials $P_k \in \mathbb{Z}[x]$ such that $\left| \bigcap_{k \geq 1} {P_k(\mathbb{Z})} \right| = \infty$.
Hint 2: How can you force $P_k(\mathbb{Z}),P_{k+1}(\mathbb{Z})$ to have a large intersection?
Hint 3: Let $P_k$ be defined as $P_0 = x,P_{k+1} = P_k(Q_k(x))$ for some $Q_k\in\mathbb{Z}[x]$. Choose suitable $Q_k$ such that $P_n(n)$ would be in $P_k(\mathbb{Z})$ for all $n,k$.

statement: Point $P$ is inside the acute triangle $\bigtriangleup ABC$ such that $\angle BPC=90^{\circ}$ and $\angle BAP=\angle PAC$. Let $D$ be the projection of $P$ onto the side $BC$. Let $M$ and $N$ be the incenters of the triangles $\bigtriangleup ABD$ and $\bigtriangleup ADC$ respectively. Prove that the quadrilateral $BMNC$ is cyclic.
Hint 1: the only way to approach $BMNC$ seems to be pop at $I$, make and check a conjecture here that will "split" $M$ and $N$ and also solve the problem.
Hint 2: we show $APMB$ cyclic. Try to delete $M$ by reducing it to an angle condition
Hint 3: the angle condition is $\angle APB=90 + {\angle ADB}/{2}$. to access $\angle ADB$, construct where the exterior angle bisector $l$ meets $BC$. This reduces the problem to proving an angle bisector, motivates extending $BP,CP$ to meet $l$.

statement:
Define glueing of positive integers as writing their base ten representations one after another and
interpreting the result as the base ten representation of a single positive integer.
Find all positive integers $k$ for which there exists an integer $N_k$ with the following property: for all $n \ge N_k$, we can glue the numbers $1,2,\dots,n$ in some order so that the result is a number divisible by $k$.
\\ \emph{Remark.} The base ten representation of a positive integer never starts with zero.
\\ \emph{Example.} Glueing $15, 14, 7$ in this order makes $15147$.
Hint 1: Find first which for sure dont work by analyzing some small cases.
Hint 2: Clearly you have way to many options what to do and dont really have control. Ristrict your choices to some subset which you can handle and which is able to handle the upcoming "noise".
Hint 3: Fix some numbers at the end, which you can permute to get any residue class.

statement: Show that there are no rational numbers \(x, y, z\) with \(x + y + z = 0\) and \(x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 100\).
Hint 2: Let \(a, b, c\) be integers with \(x = a/c\) and \(y = b/c\) (why can we do this?).
Hint 3: We get \(a^2 + ab + b^2 = 50c^2\). How do we finish?

statement: In a cyclic quadrilateral $ABCD$, let $I_1$ and $I_2$ be the incentes of $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle DBC$ respectively. Prove that $I_1I_2BC$ is a cyclic quadrilateral as well.
Hint 1: Here is an important claim: $2 \angle FBE=\angle DBA$.
Hint 2: If thats not obvious, decompose $\angle B$ into a lot of smaller angles.
Hint 3: Finally, show that $\angle FBE=\angle FCE$.
Solution: First, decompose $\angle CBA$ into 4 smaller angle, $\angle CBF, \angle FBE, \angle EBD$ and $\angle DBA$. Now notice $\angle CBF=\angle FBE+\angle EBD$ but also $\angle CBF+\angle FBE= \angle EBD+\angle DBA$. After combining these equation, one has $2\angle FBE=\angle DBA$. Similarly,  $2\angle ECF=\angle ACD$. But now that $ABCD$ is cyclic would imply $\angle DBA=\angle ACD$. This give $\angle FBE=\angle FCE$, done!

statement:
Given two relatively prime positive integers $m$ and $n$, both greater than one, show that
\[ \frac{\log_{10} m}{\log_{10} n}\]
is not a rational number.
Hint 1: start by assuming it is a rational number, what happens?
Hint 2: get rid of the logs by letting $a=\log_{10} m$ and $b=\log_{10} n$
Hint 3: use the fact they are relatively prime (i.e. they have no common prime factors) to get a contradiction
Solution: q

statement: A finite number of strips whose widths sum to $100$ and a circle of radius $1$ are given in the plane. Prove that is is possible to move each strip without rotating it, so that together the strips completely cover the circle (including its interior).
Hint 1: List the strips in order of increasing slope.
Hint 2: Place the strips in the list one at a time, each one covering an "extremal" portion of the currently uncovered area.
Hint 3: Each strip covers a portion of the perimeter larger than its width.

statement:
Let $ABC$ be a triangle with incenter $I$. Two distinct points $P$ and $Q$ are chosen on the circumcircle of $ABC$ such that
\[ \angle API = \angle AQI = 45^\circ. \]Lines $PQ$ and $BC$ meet at $S$. Let $H$ denote the foot of the altitude from $A$ to $BC$. Prove that $\angle AHI = \angle ISH$.
Hint 1: incircle invert if u want, but we will focus on elementary synth. Projecting from $I$ motivates the construction of the $A$-sharky point. This will also help delete $H$.
Hint 2: from prior hint, you should've got that $KPQM$ is a harmonic quadrilateral. Where $K$ is the $A$-sharky point and $M$ is the midpoint of minor arc $BC$. So add in the intersection of the tangents at $K$ and $M$, $X$. also notice $X,S,P,Q$ are collinear.
Hint 3: Prove that it is sufficient to prove $OX$ is parallel to $IS$. If this were true it would create a pair of homothetic triangles. Where is the centre of homothety?(finish concurrency with Appolonius)

statement:
Merlin’s castle has 100 rooms and 1000 corridors. Each corridor links two
of the rooms. Each pair of rooms is linked by at most one corridor. Merlin
has given out the plan of the castle to the wise men and declared the rules
of a challenge:
The wise men must scatter across the rooms in a manner of their
choosing. Each minute Merlin chooses a corridor and one of the
wise men must pass along that corridor from one of the rooms
joined by the corridor to the other one. Merlin wins if there are
no wise men in either room joined by the chosen corridor.
Denote a number $m$ the magic number of the castle if $m$ wise men can preagree before the challenge and act in such a way that Merlin never wins, $m$
being the minimal possible number.
What are the possible values of the magic number of the castle? (Merlin
and all the wise men always know the location of all the wise men).
Hint 1: Try find $m$ for all graphs $G$.
Hint 2: What is $m$ when $G$ is a complete graph and when $G$ is a tree.
Solution:
I will prove that for all graphs $G$, its magic number $m$ is equal to the number of 
  edges. To do this I will induct on the number of edges. Suppose that for any graph with $i<n$
  edges if it has at least $i$ wiseman in it merlin can play such that a vertex with degree $k$ 
  has $k$ wiseman in it. Also suppose that if we have a graph with $i<n$ edges that its magic number is $i$. 
  Now suppose we have a graph with $n$ edges and $n-1$ wisemen. If there is a vertex of degree $k$ with $k$ wiseman in it 
  we can remove that vertice and by the induction we get a graph with $n-1$ edges and at most $n-2$ wiseman which means that 
  a state with two adjacent vertices having no wisemen in them can be reached. Now suppose that we 
  do not have such a vertice. Remove a vertice $v$, this leaves a graph with $n-\deg(v)$ edges and 
  it has at least $n-\deg(v)$ wisemen within it. Thus we can play so that we have a vertice $k$ such 
  that it has $\deg(k)$ wiseman within it. Now add the vertice $v$ back, the vertice of degree $k$ 
  has at least $\deg(k)-1$ wisemen in it. If it has exactly $\deg(k)-1$ wisemen remove the vertice $k$, 
  preform the operation again so that we have another vertice $j$ such that $\deg(j)$ wisemen in it. 
  If $k$ and $j$ are not adjacent then when we add $k$ back $j$ still has $\deg(j)$ wisemen in it. 
  If they are adjacent we have $k$ has $\deg(k)-1$ wisemen and $j$ has $\deg(j)-1$ wisemen in it and 
  they are connected, so we force the wisemen to move along that connection and we get that 
  one of $j$ or $k$ has as many wisemen in it as its degree. Thus in all cases we can remove a vertice 
  and reach a graph that allows merlin to win so we get that $m\geq n$ where $n$ is the number 
  of edges in a graph $G$. We have a base case as it is clear that when we have one edge 
  with one wisemen it is possible to create a vertice containing as many wisemen as its degree. 
  Now to prove that the wisemen can win with as many wisemen as edges, we assign each wisemen to an edge, 
  this clearly means that a state where an edge does not a have a wisemen on either side.

statement: Let $k, M$ be positive integers such that $k-1$ is not squarefree. Prove that there exist a positive real $\alpha$, such that $\lfloor \alpha\cdot k^n \rfloor$ and $M$ are coprime for any positive integer $n$.
Hint 1: There is a rational choice of $\alpha$ that works. How can you change $\lfloor \alpha\cdot k^n \rfloor$ into something easier to handle? The floor of integers and constants are easier to handle.
Hint 2: There exists $p$ for which $p^2 \mid k-1$. Then $\alpha=N+1/p$ works for some choice of N.
Hint 3: Use modular inverses and Chinese Remainder Theorem to get a choice of $N$ that works.

statement: Two different integers $u$ and $v$ are written on a board. We perform a sequence of steps. At each step we do one of the following two operations: (i) If $a$ and $b$ are different integers on the board, then we can write $a + b$ on the board, if it is not already there. (ii) If $a$, $b$ and $c$ are three different integers on the board, and if an integer $x$ satisfies $ax^2 +bx+c = 0$, then we can write $x$ on the board, if it is not already there. Determine all pairs of starting numbers $(u, v)$ from which any integer can eventually be written on the board after a finite sequence of steps.
Hint 1: Note that for any $u,v$, we can get $ux^2+(u+v)x+v=0$ and $x=-1$ as a root
Hint 2: Prove the $u=0, v=0$ and $u,v < 0$ cases don't work
Hint 3: Check the$v=1$ and $v > 1$ case

Hint 1: Consider consecutive elements $u,v,w$ in the circle. What are the possible divisibility relations between $u,v,$ and $w$, subject to both constraints (a) and (b)? What can you deduce about the possible sizes of the circle?
Hint 2: Either $n=3$ or $n$ is even. You now need to provide constructions of such rings for these values of $n$: try something involving primes and products of primes, keeping in mind what you figured out from hint 1.

Source: Athemath F24 application pset, P3 of 6
Hint 1: consider the expected sum of each number independently.

statement:
Let $ABC$ be an acute-angled scalene triangle with incircle $\omega$ and circumcircle $\Gamma$. Suppose $\omega$ touches line $BC$ at $D$ and the tangent to $\Gamma$ at $A$ meets line $BC$ at $T$. Two circles passing through $A$ and $T$ tangent to $\omega$ meet line $AD$ again at $X$ and $Y$.

Prove that $BXCY$ is a trapezium.
Hint 1: We would like to delete the circles so as they both pass through $A,T $ a $\sqrt{bc}$ invert (a.k.a. force overlay) will delete them and turn them into tangent lines instead, however if you are not familiar with mixtilinear excircle, don't worry, by an "extraversion" (an equivalence between incircle and excircle config) you can state the inverted problem for mixtilinear incircle and don't worry, inverted $T$ (or just $T'$) is fixed in the "extraversion".
Hint 2: Let the mixtilinear incircle touch $(ABC)$ at $T_A$ and touch sides $AB, AC$ at $F,E$ respectively, and let $D$ a point in $AT'$ such that $T_AD$ bisects $\angle AT_AT'$, extend $DE, DF$ and let them hit the mixtilinear incircle again at $Y_1, X_1$ respectively. By using two degen DITs (focusing on the fact that the invertion swapping the involutions preservers the circle) on mixtilinear incircle, what is the pole of $X_1Y_1$ w.r.t. the mixtilinear incircle?
Hint 3: Notice that by ghost points all you need to prove is $(A, T_A; B, C)=(A, T_A; Y, X)$, so we will work towards projecting $(A, T_A; Y, X)$, starting by a pole transform w.r.t. the mixtilinear incircle and now, note that line $T_AD$ goes through a point in the mixtilinear incircle whose tangent to it is parallel to $BC$, we consider the antipode of this point and then focus on getting as much propeties as we can with Brokard on $X_1FY_1E$ and swapping involution pairs in the mixtilinear incircle.

statement: Find the least positive integer \(M\) for which there exist a positive integer \(n\) and polynomials \(P_1(x)\), \(P_2(x)\), \(\ldots\), \(P_n(x)\) with integer coefficients satisfying\[Mx=P_1(x)^3+P_2(x)^3+\cdots+P_n(x)^3.\]
Hint 1: There is an example for $M=6$: $6x=(x+1)^3+(x-1)^3+(-x)^3+(-x)^3$. To prove $M$ must be a least $6$ It's enough to prove that it must be divisible by $6$.
Hint 2: For every polynomial $P$ with integer coefficients, the coefficient of $x$ in $P^3$ is divisible by $3$. Can you do something similar to prove that $M$ must be even?
Hint 3: Prove that for every $P$ with integer coefficients, the sum of the coefficients of powers of the form $x^{3k+1}$ and $x^{3k+2}$ at the polynomial $P^3$ is even.

statement: Point $P$ lies on the side $CD$ of the cyclic quadrilateral $ABCD$ such that $\angle CBP = 90^{\circ}$. Let $K$ be the intersection of $AC,BP$ such that $AK = AP = AD$. $H$ is the projection of $B$ onto the line $AC$. Prove that $\angle APH = 90^{\circ}$.
Hint 1: the condition seems hard to work with and the diagram seems hard to draw. it's easy to reduce the problem to showing $HB=HP$.
Hint 2: power of a point, 90 degree thales theorem from $HB=HP$  and other things motivate reflecting $B$ over $AC$
Hint 3: $B'PAC$ cyclic, use this to angle chase the angle $BPB'$

Source: 2023 Austria MO, Preliminary Round, P4 of 4
statement: Find all pairs of positive integers $(n,k)$ such that $$n!+n=n^k$$
Hint 1: Prove that $n$ must be prime
Hint 2: Consider the equation $$(n-2)!=n^{k-2}+n^{k-3}+\cdots +n+1$$ What mod is useful to consider?
Hint 3: Try $\mod {n-1}$

statement: Given are two parallelograms \(ABCD\) and \(AECF\) with common diagonal \(AC\), where \(E\) and \(F\) lie inside the parallelogram \(ABCD\). Prove that the circumcircles of the triangles \(AEB\), \(BFC\), \(CED\) and \(DFA\) pass through a common point.
Hint 1: Define point \(H\) to be the intersection of the circumcircles of \(AEB\) and \(CED\) and prove that it also lies on the two other circles.

statement:
A \emph{disphenoid} is a tetrahedron whose triangular face is congurent to each other. What is the least total surface area of a disphenoid whose faces are scalene triangle with integer side lengths?

\emph{Note: A triangle that has pairwise distinct side lengths is known as a scalene triangle.}
Hint 1: It is very tempted to say a $2-3-4$ triangle works, but it cannot form a tetraherdron. Why?
Hint 2: If you just stick 4 congurent triangular faces together, it might not form a tetrahedron. One cases is when the triangles are obtuse.

statement: What is the product of the real roots of the equation\[x^2 + 18x + 30 = 2 \sqrt{x^2 + 18x + 45}\,\,?\]
Hint 1: Notice the similarity between terms inside and outside the square root.
Hint 2: Hint 1 motivates a substitution.
Hint 3: Let $y = x^2 + 18x + 30$. (Other substitutions also work, such as $z = x^2 + 18x + 45$, $t = \sqrt{x^2 + 18x + 45}$.

statement: Let $P$ be a regular $99$-gon. Assign integers between $1$ and $99$ to the vertices of $P$ such that each integer appears exactly once.  (If two assignments coincide under rotation, treat them as the same. )  An \textit{operation} is a swap of the integers assigned to a pair of adjacent vertices of $P$. Find the smallest integer $n$ such that one can achieve every other assignment from a given one with no more than $n$ operations.
Hint 1: For the upper bound, use 1-99 going clockwise and 1-99 going anticlockwise. Prove that among any triple of integers, at least one pair must be swapped at some point.

statement: For positive integral $k>1$, we let $p(k)$ be its smallest prime divisor. Given an integer $a_1>2$, we define an infinite sequence $a_n$ by $a_{n+1}=a_n^n-1$ for each $n\geq 1$. For which values of $a_1$ is the sequence $p(a_n)$ bounded?
Hint 1: Show that if $a_1$ is odd $p(a_n)$ is indeed bounded.
Hint 2: In the other case, try to apply Fermat's little theorem.

statement: A game is played between two players, $A$ and $B$. First $A$ picks a directed graph with at least $2$ vertices such that if $u$ is connected to $v$ then $v$ isn't connected to $u$. Then $B$ picks the starting positions for $A$ and $B$, which must be in different positions. Now the game starts on $A$'s move, and then alternates between them. On $A$'s move, if she is at a vertex $v$ with a path from $u$ to $v$, she can move to $u$. If she has no legal moves, she loses. On $B$'s turn, he moves along a path (but in the right direction). If he has no legal moves he stays in place. If $A$ and $B$ are ever in the same vertex, $B$ wins. If A never gets caught, she wins. Who wins?

Hint 1: Split the board up in $3\times 3$ sub-boards. Prove that the gardener can ensure that in each of them are 5 majestatic trees.
Hint 2: For the lumberjack, you want to assign him trees that he protects always, when the gardener grows them. Find such a coloring of the board which satiesfies that.
Hint 3: You want to have the same coloring in each $3\times 3$ sub-board, which we defined before. There arent too many, just bash them out until you find a fitting one.

statement: Let $n$ be a positive integer. In each cell of an $n \times n$ grid, we place a coin with either heads or tails facing upwards. Each time we can select one cell, and flip all the $2n − 1$ coins that lie in the same row or the same column as this cell. Find all $n$ such that it is always possible to have all the coins with the heads facing upwards after finitely many moves, no matter how we place the coins initially.
Hint 1: $n$ is 1 or $n$ is even.

statement: The numbers $1,2,3,\dots,2017$ are on the blackboard. Amelie and Boris take turns removing one of those until only two numbers remain on the board. Amelie starts. If the sum of the last two numbers is divisible by $8$, then Amelie wins. Else Boris wins. Who can force a victory?
Hint 1: Notice Amelie gets the last move: if we can choose each of her moves such that the sum of all the numbers becomes divisible by 8, she will win.
Hint 2: Try pairing each number with another such that the sum of each pair is divisible by 8 (save for one number which can't be paired as 2017 is odd).

statement: Let $ABCD$ be a parallelogram and points $E,F$ lie outside $ABCD$. If triangles $BCF$ and $DEC$ are similar, i.e. $\triangle BCF \sim \triangle DEC$, prove that triangle $AEF$ is similar to these two triangles.
Hint 1: Prove $\triangle FBA \sim \triangle FCE$
Hint 2: Rearrange the similarity conditions to find the desired similarity

statement:
Tony Wang starts with $n$ cards labeled $1,2,3,\dots,n$ lined up in a random order on his desk. He calls a pair $(a,b)$ of these cards swapped if $a>b$ and the card labeled $a$ is to the left of the card labeled $b$. For instance, in the sequence of cards $3,1,4,2$, there are three swapped pairs of cards, $(3,1)$, $(3,2)$, and $(4,2)$.

He picks up the card labeled 1 and inserts it back into the sequence in the opposite position: if the card labeled 1 had $i$ card to its left, then it now has $i$ cards to its right. He then picks up the card labeled $2$ and reinserts it in the same manner, and so on until he has picked up and put back each of the cards $1,2,\dots,n$ exactly once in that order. (For example, the process starting at $3,1,4,2$ would be $3,1,4,2 \to 3,4,1,2 \to 2,3,4,1 \to 2,4,3,1 \to 2,3,4,1$.)

Show that, no matter what lineup of cards Tony Wang started with, his final lineup has the same number of swapped pairs as the starting lineup.
Hint 1: Note that I'll be giving hints based on MY solution, as the official solution is too unmotivatable for my taste. \newline Call two ordered sets of cards $A,B$ \textit{adjacent} if $A$ can be derived from swapping exactly one adjacent pair of cards in $B$, and vice versa. For example, 124356 and 123456 are adjacent, but 125436 and 123456 are not. \newline What observations can we make about this property?
Hint 2: Denote each individual operation where Tony Wang moves the card labeled $i$ as $F_i$ (and denote $F_i (A)$ the ordered set of cards derived from applying $F_i$ on an ordered set of cards $A$). Prove that if $A,B$ are adjacent then $F_i(A), F_i(B)$ are adjacent.
Hint 3: Calling the entire operation from start to finish $F$, and defining $d(A)$ as the minimal number of swaps between adjacent pairs required to derive the ordered set $S := (1,2,3,…,n)$ from $A$, which is also the number of inversions in A. Since $F(S) = S$, this implies that $d(F(A)) \leq d(A)$. \newline \newline The final step is to show that this value isn't just a monovariant, but an invariant!

statement:
Let $n \geqslant 3$ be an integer, and let $x_1,x_2,\ldots,x_n$ be real numbers in the interval $[0,1]$. Let $s=x_1+x_2+\ldots+x_n$, and assume that $s \geqslant 3$. Prove that there exist integers $i$ and $j$ with $1 \leqslant i<j \leqslant n$ such that
\[2^{j-i}x_ix_j>2^{s-3}.\]
Hint 1: Consider the pair $(i, j)$ such that $2^{j-i}x_ix_j$ is maximal.
Hint 2: Determine a bound on $s$ in terms of this pair.

statement:
A triangle $ABC$ with $AB < AC$ has circumcenter $O$. The perpendicular bisector of $BC$ meets line $AB,AC$ at $D,E$ respectively. The circles with diameters $DE,AO$ meet at two points $X,Y$. Let $M$ be the midpoint of $BC$.

Prove that the circumcircle of triangle $MXY$ is tangent to the circumcircle of triangle $ABC$.
Hint 1: It's not too hard to show that the circle with diameter $DE$ is orthogonal to $(ABC$). This as well as 2 random tangent circles motivate inversion around one of these circles
Hint 2: inversion around $(ABC)$ gives a fairly sus problem that turns out to be solvable but not a good inversion. Focus on inversion wrt the circle with diameter $DE$
Hint 3: prove that $(MXY)$ inverts to the circle with diameter $AO$.

statement: Let $\mathbb{N}_0$ denote the set of non-negative integers. Determine all non-negative integers $k$ for which there exists a function $f: \mathbb{N}_0 \to \mathbb{N}_0$ such that $f(2024) = k$ and $$f(f(n)) \leq f(n+1) - f(n)$$ for all non-negative integers $n$.
Hint 1: Prove that $f$ must be weakly increasing.
Hint 2: Generally speaking now, the expression $ff(n)$ grows way faster than $f$ by feeling if $ff(n)>0$ for some $n$. Try to prove that $ff(n)=0$ for all $n$ is necessary.
Hint 3: Note that from the previous hint we also get that $f(n)<n$. For construction you need to have $ff(n)=0$, what motivates this?

statement: Determine all pairs \((x, y)\) of integers with \((x+2)^4 - x^4 = y^3\).
Hint 1: The equation is equivalent to \((x+1)^3 + (x+1) = (y/2)^3\).
Hint 2: Prove that for any integer \(a\), \(a^3 + a\) is only a cube if and only if \(a=0\).
Hint 3: Show Hint 2 by proving \(a^3 + a < (a+1)^3\) for positive \(a\) and \(a^3 + a > (a-1)^3\) for negative \(a\).

statement: Find 3 even number less than $400$, such that each number can be expressed as a sum of consecutive positive odd numbers in at least 6 different ways.
Hint 1: Suppose $a=(n)+(n+2)+...(n+2k)$. Try to simplify this arithmetic sequence.
Hint 2: Vary $k$ as shown in hint 1. Now, if you need at least 6 different ways to express $a$, how many factors do you need for $a$?
Hint 3: $a$ need at least 12 factors. Rest is just bashing.

statement:
Show that there exists a set of $7$ points on a plane such that, for any set of three lines, there is a region bounded by the lines containing at least two points.

\textit{Note that a region includes its boundary and can be infinite or finite.}
Hint 1: Draw $7$ lines at random and try to see why why it works/doesn't work
Hint 2: The correct construction is when all the points lie on one circle
Hint 3: Three lines can only cut a circle at most $6$ times
Solution: If those $7$ points lie on one circle, there would need to be $7$ cuts of the circle to separate them. However, since a line can only cut a circle twice, $3$ lines can only cut the circle $6$ times, thus proving that at least two points are inside one region

statement: A token is placed at each vertex of a regular $2n$-gon. A \textit{move} consists in choosing an edge of the $2n$-gon and swapping the two tokens placed at the endpoints of that edge. After a _x000c_finite number of moves have been performed, it turns out that every two tokens have been swapped exactly once. Prove that some edge has never been chosen.
Hint 1: Intuitively, we want to say something about the relative positions of the tokens. Since they are arranged in a circle, comparing relative positions of 2 tokens does not make sense, but what about the "orientation" of 3 tokens?
Hint 2: Prove that every triangle of tokens changes orientation at the end of the process. For example if tokens were originally 1,2,3 going clockwise around the circle, 1,3,2  going clockwise would be considered the opposite orientation. Hence, show that the net effect of this process is a reflection about some axis of symmetry of the $2n$-gon. That is, show that there exists a line such that the final position of each token is the reflection of its initial position about this line
Hint 3: Consider the two edges perpendicular to the axis of symmetry mentioned in hint 2. Look at the parity of the number of times that each token swaps over these two edges.

Hint 1: Let $(a,k)=1$. Prove either $f(a)\geq 1$ or the required sum is non-negative.
Hint 2: Let $p_1, \dots, p_n$ be the primes $\mid k$ with $f(p_i)<0$. Consider summing $f(x)$ over $x$ coprime to $p_1, \dots, p_i$, for each $i$.
Hint 3: Use induction to show that such a sum is negative for each $i$, assuming the base case $\sum_{x=1}^k f(x)<0$.

statement:
There are $1001$ stacks of coins $S_1, S_2, \dots, S_{1001}$. Initially, stack $S_k$ has $k$ coins for each $k = 1,2,\dots,1001$. In an operation, one selects an ordered pair $(i,j)$ of indices $i$ and $j$ satisfying $1 \leq i < j \leq 1001$ subject to two conditions:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
    \item The stacks $S_i$ and $S_j$ must each have at least $1$ coin.
    \item The ordered pair $(i,j)$ must \textit{not} have been selected before.
\end{enumerate}
Then, if $S_i$ and $S_j$ have $a$ coins and $b$ coins respectively, one removes $\gcd(a,b)$ coins from each stack.

What is the maximum number of times this operation could be performed?
Hint 1: Separate the stacks into two groups and prove the maximum on each
Hint 2: Separate $S_1 \dots S_{500}$ into one group and the remaining into another
Hint 3: For the construction, try finishing each stack separately form the end.
Solution:
The answer is $500 \cdot 501 = 250500$. Our solution is split into two parts. Firstly, we will
give a valid construction.
We start by performing operations $(1001, 1000), (1001, 999), \dots , (1001, 1)$, in order. By
induction, at each step $(1001, j)$, $S_{1001}$ will have $j+1$ coins and thus, since $\gcd(j+1, j) = 1$,
one coin will be removed from each stack. At the end of this process, $1000$ operations will
have been performed. $S_{1001}$ will have one coin; we discard it. The remaining (nonempty)
stacks will have $1, 2, \cdots , 999$ coins, and no operation will have been performed between
any of them. Thus we can repeat this process, performing operations with the $999$-coin
stack and the rest of the stacks in descending order. Repeating this process until all the
stacks have been discarded, we perform $1000 + 998 + \dots + 2 = 500 \cdot 501$ operations, as
desired.
To prove this is the maximum number of operations we can perform, we bound the
total number of operations. The stacks $S_1, \dots, S_{500}$ can only participate in at most
$1 + · · · + 500 = 500 \cdot 501/2$ operations (since each operation removes at least one coin
from them). The remaining $501$ stacks can only perform ${501 \choose 2} = 500 \cdot 501/2$ operations
between themselves, since each pair can only perform the operation once. Thus, in total,
we can perform at most $500 \cdot 501$ operations

statement: Let $ABC$ be a triangle with $AB \neq AC$ and circumcenter $O$. The bisector of $\angle BAC$ intersects $BC$ at $D$. Let $E$ be the reflection of $D$ with respect to the midpoint of $BC$. The lines through $D$ and $E$ perpendicular to $BC$ intersect the lines $AO$ and $AD$ at $X$ and $Y$ respectively. Prove that the quadrilateral $BXCY$ is cyclic.
Hint 1: Consider reflecting $Y$ about the perpendicular bisector of $BC$.
Hint 2: It's sufficient to show $BXCY'$ is cyclic.
Hint 3: You can prove the prior hint with PoP

statement: Prove that there is no positive integer $n$ such that, for $k = 1,2,\ldots,9$, the leftmost digit (in decimal notation) of $(n+k)!$ equals $k$.
Hint 1: Suppose that such an integer $n$ exists. for each $1\leq k\leq 9$, there exists an integer $p_{k}$ such that: $k.10^{p_{k}}\leq  (n+k)! < (k+1).10^{p_{k}}$

statement: Show that $\tan\left(1^{\circ}\right)$ is irrational.
Hint 1: Try contradiction.
Hint 2: By assumption, $\tan\left(1^{\circ}\right)$ is rational. Show that this implies $\tan\left(n^{\circ}\right)$ is rational for all positive integers $n<90$.
Solution: Suppose that $\tan\left(1^{\circ}\right)$ is rational. We will show by induction that $\tan\left(n^{\circ}\right)$ is also rational, for all positive integers $n<90$. The base case $n=1$ is by assumption, so suppose that $\tan\left(k^{\circ}\right)$ is rational for some arbitrary $k<89$. It follows that $\tan\left(k+1^{\circ}\right)$ is rational since $\tan\left(k+1^{\circ}\right)=\frac{\tan\left(k^{\circ}\right)+\tan\left(1^{\circ}\right)}{1-\tan\left(k^{\circ}\right)\tan\left(1^{\circ}\right)}$, of which is clearly rational, since all components are rational, which completes the induction. But then this implies that $\tan\left(60^{\circ}\right)=\sqrt{3}$ is rational, contradiction.

statement: \begin{enumerate}\item[(a)]{Find the maximum value of the expression \begin{equation*}x^2y - y^2x\end{equation*} when $0 \le x \le 1$ and $0 \le y \le 1$.}\item[(b)]{Find the maximum value of the expression \begin{equation*}x^2y + y^2z + z^2x - x^2z - y^2x - z^2y\end{equation*} when $0 \le x \le 1$, $0 \le y \le 1$, $0 \le z \le 1$.}\end{enumerate}
Hint 1: For (a), what happens as you increase x?
Hint 2: For (b), take wlog $z$ the smallest of the three, and analyse the terms involving $z$.

statement: Let $ABC$ be a triangle with its vertices labeled counterclockwise, and let $P,Q,R,S$ be the unique points such that $APQB$ and $ARSC$ are squares outside the triangle $ABC$. Let $X = BR \cap CP, Y = BS \cap CQ, K = (BQY) \cap (CSY)$. Prove that $KX$ passes through the midpoint of $BC$.
Hint 1: K is the spiral similarity sending $BP$ to $SC$ - but wait, it sends the entire squares to each other as well! What resources do we get here?
Hint 2: Knowing that $K$ also sends $QA$ to $AR$, we are motivated to take a root bc inversion at $A$, which swaps $Q,R$ and sends $K$ to the reflection of $A$ over the midpoint of $QR$ - but this means that in the pre-inversion diagram, the reflection $L$ of $A$ over $K$ satisfies $(Q,R;L,A) = -1$! (Or alternatively, we can instantly derive this through the dumpty point lemma, I'm just showing that even without knowing this theory this move can still be motivated).
Hint 3: Reflect $A$ over the midpoint $M$ of BC to get $T$. Now we need to show that $LT // XM$. Now, find a second spiral similarity.

statement: Let $x$ and $y$ be positive integers. If ${x^{2^n}}-1$ is divisible by $2^ny+1$ for every positive integer $n$, prove that $x=1$.
Hint 1: Consider which primes can divide $x^{2^n}-1$.
Hint 2: Consider only considering $2^ny+1$ and which primes divide it once you have made the observation relevant to hint 1.

Source: Mocktest Geometry Olympiad MGO 2024, P1 of 6
statement:
Let $I$ be the incenter of $\triangle ABC$. Let $l$ be an arbitary line
passing through $I$. $l$ intersects $(ABC)$ at points $X$ and $Y$ and intersects $BC$ at
$Z$. The line through $Z$ parallel to $AI$ intersects $AX$ and $AY$ at $D$ and $E$ respectively.
Prove that points $A, I, D, E$ are concyclic.
Hint 1: focus on triangle $ADE$
Hint 2: miquel point with $XYZ$, it lies on $ZM$ and many circles
Hint 3: use the miquel cyclic quads and $MI^{2}=MT \times MZ$ to angle chase, where T is the miquel point or ($ADE$) int ($ABC$)

statement: We are given an acute triangle $ABC$ with $AB\neq AC$. Let $D$ be a point of $BC$ such that $DA$ is tangent to the circumcircle of $ABC$. Let $E$ and $F$ be the circumcenters of triangles $ABD$ and $ACD$, respectively, and let $M$ be the midpoints $EF$. Prove that the line tangent to the circumcircle of $AMD$ through $D$ is also tangent to the circumcircle of $ABC$.
Hint 1: Introduce the center $O$ of $ABC$ what special properties does it have?
Hint 2: Prove that $DEFO$ is a parallelogram, consider the second intersection $X$ of the $A$ symmedian with circumcircle $ABC$, what can you tell about $M$.
Hint 3: $M$ is the circumcenter of $DAOX$.

statement: There are $n \geq 2$ control points. We want to build $k$ roads such that each road connects two different control points directly and no two roads connect the same pair of control points. Find the smallest $k$ (in terms of $n$) such that no matter how we build the roads, we can always go from any control point to any other control point by travelling along one or two roads.
Hint 1: Suppose there exists two control points \(A\) and \(B\) that cannot reach each other within 2 roads, there cannot exist \(P \neq A, B\) such that \(AP\) and \(PB\) are both roads.
Solution: Answer: $k = \binom{n-1}{2}+1$\\ Construction:\\ Pick $n-1$ control points and build all roads between them. Since the last control point is isolated, it can never be reached from other control points.\\ Proof of bound:\\ Say there exists two control points $A$ and $B$ that cannot reach each other within one or two roads. Let $S$ be the set of control points that are not $A$ nor $B$. For each $s \in S$, at least one of $As$ and $sB$ is not a road, or else the path $A$ to $s$ to $B$ would be valid. Furthermore, $AB$ cannot be a road, or else the path $A$ to $B$ would be valid. Since $|S| = n - 2$, we get the following bound:\\ $$k \leq \binom{n}{2} - (n-2) - 1 = \binom{n-1}{2},$$\\ which is what we have claimed.

statement: Let $P$ be a point on the circumcircle of square $ABCD$. Suppose $PA \cdot PC=56$ and $PB \cdot PD=90$. What is the area of $ABCD$?
Hint 1: Note that $AC$ and $BD$ is diameter of the square $ABCD$.
Hint 2: Consider a point $X$, such that $PX \perp AC$.
Hint 3: Express $PX$ in terms of area of $APC$.

statement:
All the chairs in a classroom are arranged in a square $n\times n$ array (in other words, $n$ columns and $n$ rows), and every chair is occupied by a student. The teacher decides to rearrange the students according to the following two rules:
\begin{enumerate}[(a)]
\item Every student must move to a new chair.
\item A student can only move to an adjacent chair in the same row or to an adjacent chair in the same column. In other words, each student can move only one chair horizontally or vertically.
\end{enumerate}
(Note that the rules above allow two students in adjacent chairs to exchange places.)
Show that this procedure can be done if $n$ is even, and cannot be done if $n$ is odd.
Hint 1: Check what stays as an invariant on the odd array and why?

statement: Let $a_1,a_2,a_3,\dots$ be an infinite sequence of non-zero reals satisfying \[a_{i} = \frac{a_{i-1}a_{i-2}-2}{a_{i-3}}\]for all $i\geq 4$. Determine all positive integers $n$ such that if $a_1,a_2,\dots,a_n$ are integers, then all elements of the sequence are integers.
Hint 1: First, consider the case where all the elements of the sequence are odd. Show that if the first 6 terms are integers, then all of them are.
Hint 2: However, we still need to deal with $2$. Prove that the only way for the subsequent term not to be an integer (assuming the first 6 terms are) is if we have an even term followed by two odd terms. Bearing this in mind, can you come up with an example showing $n=6$ does not work?
Hint 3: Prove that if the first $8$ terms are integers, then this is impossible. Try considering $v_2$ and focus on the $v_2$ of $a_4$

statement:
Let $n>1$ be an integer. Alice and Bob take turns marking unmarked points on the unit circle. Alice starts and marks her points red, while Bob marks his blue. They stop once each player has marked exactly $n$ points. Each player's \textit{score} is defined as the length of the longest arc of the circle which has both endpoints of that player's color and which doesn't contain any marked points in its interior, or $0$ if such an arc doesn't exist.

If the scores are equal it's a tie, and otherwise the player with the higher score wins. Does one of the players have a winning strategy?
Hint 1: Bob has a winning strategy. Try finding it by first considering small cases, like $n=2,3$.
Hint 2: Complete Alice's first move to a regular $n$-gon inscribed in the circle.
Hint 3: Let Bob mark as many vertices of this $n$-gon as possible. What happens next?
Solution:
The answer is that Bob can always win. After Alice's first move, we construct the regular $n$-gon inscribed in the circle that has the first marked point as a vertex. Firstly, Bob simply marks vertices of that $n$-gon until there are no unmarked ones left. Suppose he managed to mark $k$ points of the polygon, so Alice marked $n-k$ points.\\
\\
Note that $n>k>0$ (the strict inequalities are important). This means that there are at most $n-k-1$ pairs of consecutive red vertices of the polygon. In Bob's next $n-k-1$ moves, he marks a point in each small arc of the circle bounded by two consecutive red vertices of the polygon. He can make sure each such arc contains a blue point. After this, each of Alice's potential arcs has length strictly less then $1/n$ of the perimeter of the circle.\\
\\
Call an arc of the original polygon "good" if at least one of its endpoints is a marked blue point. These are all arcs which don't have both endpoints red, so there are at least $n-(n-k-1)=k+1$ of them. Because Alice can only mark $k$ points that are not vertices of the polygon, Alice must leave some good arc without red interior points. On his last turn, Bob can mark a blue point sufficiently close to one of the endpoints of that arc, creating a blue edge longer than any of Alice's potential winning edges, and winning.

statement: There are several discs whose radii are no more that $1$, and whose centers all lie on a segment with length ${l}$. Prove that the union of all the discs has a perimeter not exceeding $4l+8$.
Hint 1: The bound $4l+8$ is clearly not tight, what would be the best bound and its equality case?
Hint 2: Try to reduce every config to the equality case (the one where the discs are all tangent to each other).

statement:
Determine all real numbers $\alpha$ such that, for every positive integer $n$, the integer \[ \lfloor\alpha \rfloor + \lfloor 2 \alpha \rfloor + \cdots + \lfloor n \alpha \rfloor \] is a multiple of $n$.

(Note that $\lfloor z \rfloor$ denotes the greatest integer less than or equal to $z$. For example, $\lfloor -\pi \rfloor = -4$ and $\lfloor 2 \rfloor= \lfloor 2.9 \rfloor = 2$.)
Hint 1: Estimate all the floors up and down, what do you notice?
Hint 2: The sum lies in an intervall of length $n$, so if you choose the multiple of $n$ to be at the higher end, then all the fractional parts must be very small.
Hint 3: For $\alpha$ irrational apply equidistribution theorem.

statement: In triangle $ABC$, $\angle B=90^\circ$, $AB>BC$, and $P$ is the point such that $BP=BC$ and $\angle APB=90^\circ$, where $P$ and $C$ lie on the same side of $AB$. Let $Q$ be the point on $AB$ such that $AP=AQ$, and let $M$ be the midpoint of $QC$. Prove that the line through $M$ parallel to $AP$ passes through the midpoint of $AB$.
